_readme: FreeMicro pad configuration: what every input does, plus LED colours.
Edit it in a browser with `freemicro config --web`, or by hand after
`freemicro keys --init`. Either way it is reloaded while `freemicro run`
is running. `freemicro keys --dry-run` prints the id of whatever you press.
Input ids: AG00-AG05 (Agent Keys), ACT06-ACT12 (action keys), ENC_CLK /
ENC_CW / ENC_CC (dial), JOY_UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT (thumbstick flicks).
The bindings below match the SIX KEYCAPS THE PAD SHIPS WITH - FAST, APPR,
REJ, SPLIT, MIC, CODEX - so every key does what the cap in your hand says.
Swapped a cap? Change "keycaps" below and the web UI redraws the pad.
Nothing here types words into your agent, and nothing assumes an app you
may not own.
CHORDS. A binding key with a + in it binds the two keys pressed TOGETHER,
e.g. "ACT07+ACT08". Order does not matter. Two keys only; not the dial
detents or the joystick, which have no release. A key that is in a chord
AND bound on its own waits 45ms ("chords": {"settle_ms": 45}) to see if a
partner is coming; give one key {"action": "none"} and the chord costs
nothing at all. Nothing below is chorded, so nothing below waits.
While a 'hold' key is down - dictation, once you bind one on MIC - other
keys STOP TYPING. It is holding real modifier keys, so anything they
typed would come out as a shortcut instead. The skipped press is printed.
Focus, mouse and shell keys still work, because modifiers cannot change
what those mean.
Full reference: docs/CUSTOMIZING.md

AG00.label: agent 1
comment: Agent Keys 1-6: one project each. The key's colour is that project's
state (blue thinking, amber waiting on you, green just finished, red
error, dark = nothing there), and pressing it brings that project's
terminal tab to the front. Which project sits on which key is the
'agent_keys' section at the bottom of this file.
It focuses a window - it does NOT type into the current one. Nothing
happens if the project is not running, or if its terminal cannot be
identified; `freemicro keys --dry-run` prints exactly which it is.
Want one key nailed to one repo forever, whatever the policy says?
{"action": "focus_session", "project": "~/code/api"}.

ACT06.label: FAST - cycle permission mode
comment: The FAST cap's factory command is composer.toggleFastMode. Claude
Code's equivalent of 'stop stopping and go' is Shift+Tab, which cycles
normal -> auto-accept edits -> plan mode. It goes to the window you
are looking at, like every plain 'key' binding.

ACT07.label: APPR - approve (hold for always)
comment: THE AMBER KEY IS THE KEY THAT ANSWERS IT. When a project goes amber
it is blocked on a permission prompt; this key answers that prompt in
THAT project - it raises that terminal tab first and sends the answer
there, so it never types into whatever window you happen to be in.
Tap = Yes, once. HOLD for half a second = 'Yes, and don't ask again',
which is the prompt's second option when it offers one. Nothing at
all happens when no project is waiting: this key cannot send a
keystroke on spec. `freemicro keys --list` says what it would do now.
Two projects waiting? It answers the one that asked most recently and
says so. Add "project": "~/code/api" to nail it to one repo.
Prefer two keys to a hold? Drop "long_press" and add a chord:
"ACT07+ACT08": {"action": "answer_permission", "answer": "always"}.

ACT08.label: REJ - decline
comment: The other half of ACT07, and the REJ cap's factory command
(approval.decline). Sends Escape into the asking session's tab, which
is how that prompt is declined. Same rule: no prompt, no keystroke.

ACT09.label: SPLIT - new tab in this project
comment: The SPLIT cap forks the thread on a stock pad. A new terminal tab
inherits the current one's directory, so this is 'start a second
agent on the same repo' - which is what the Agent Keys are for.

ACT10.label: MIC - pick your dictation app
comment: Unbound on purpose. Dictation shortcuts belong to an app you may not
have: `freemicro start` asks which one you use and writes the right
combo, and the web UI offers the same choices. A guessed shortcut for
an app you do not own is a key that silently does nothing.
Push-to-talk, once you have picked one, looks like:
{"action": "hold", "key": "ctrl+cmd+o"} - and set the SAME combo
in the dictation app as hold-to-talk, not toggle.

ACT11.label: MIC (second half - intentionally ignored)
ACT11.comment: The wide MIC cap spans two switches and the pad reports both. Binding both fires the key twice.

ACT12.label: CODEX - send
ACT12.comment: The CODEX cap's factory command is composer.submit. Send.

ENC_CLK.label: dial press - click
ENC_CLK.comment: Clicks wherever the pointer is - pairs with the thumbstick, which moves it.

ENC_CW.label: dial cw, up / previous
comment: Factory parity: clockwise is 'previous control or option', i.e.
ArrowUp (docs/FACTORY-DEFAULTS.md 5). Arrows move the selection in
any Claude Code picker - including a permission prompt - and walk
prompt history otherwise, so one detent is always exactly one step
and nothing can be submitted by accident.

_comment: The pad reports the stick as an angle (0..1 of a full turn) and a
distance (0..1), returning to exactly {a:0,d:0} when you let go.
mode 'pointer' (default) makes it an analogue cursor, like the red
TrackPoint nub on a ThinkPad: how far you push sets the cursor's SPEED,
not how far it jumps, and it keeps moving while you hold it. The four
JOY_* bindings below do not fire in this mode - they are kept so that
switching to 'directions' still does something sensible.
mode 'directions' is the older behaviour: one discrete flick per push,
fired when distance crosses 'deadzone' and re-armed when it falls back.
'directions' lists the four input ids starting at angle 0 and stepping
by a quarter turn in increasing-angle order - so right, down, left, up,
which is the order the hardware itself uses (angle 0.75 is UP; macOS
screen y grows downward). 'origin' rotates the whole wheel if your
stick's zero is somewhere else.
Two deadzones, on purpose. 'deadzone' (0.6) guards an ACTION and is
large because crossing it types into your terminal.
'pointer_deadzone' (0.1) only rejects the stick's own slop, and every
bit of travel above it is precision you can feel. Do not raise it to
0.6: that is how pointing ends up feeling dead.
Tuning: max_speed is pixels per second at FULL deflection - lower it if
the cursor overshoots. gamma shapes the middle of the range: 1 is
linear and twitchy, 2 is TrackPoint-like, 3 is very gentle near centre.
tick_hz is how often the cursor moves, not how often the pad reports.
Set precision_key to a spare input id (e.g. 'ACT12') and hold it to
drop to precision_scale of full speed for pixel work.
invert_y flips up/down if pointing comes out upside down on your unit.
Run `freemicro keys --dry-run` and watch the live angle, distance and
px/s while you push the stick - that is how these numbers get chosen.
enabled=false by default: opt in with `freemicro lights --enable`.
Colours are the exact factory integers from docs/FACTORY-DEFAULTS.md.
Note 'done' is the factory's UNREAD colour - it is meant to clear once
you look at the session, which state.done_ttl_seconds in config.json
does for you (default 180s). Leave it green forever and the pad stops
matching factory behaviour within minutes.
on_exit=off blanks the pad when FreeMicro stops, handing the device
back cleanly - the same thing the vendor app does on quit.
method: 'rgbcfg' uses v.oai.rgbcfg for the underglow and backlight
verified on hardware and what the vendor app uses. 'preview' uses
lights.preview, which firmware v0.4.1 accepts and ignores: it lights
nothing. Only switch to it if you are debugging the protocol.
Which project each of the six Agent Keys follows. A key stands for a
PROJECT DIRECTORY, not a session id: terminal tabs come and go, but the
repo you are working in does not, so your pad never needs re-teaching.
policy:
recent  (default) the six most recently active projects fill the keys
automatically, newest first. Nothing to configure.
pinned  slots you name below keep their key; the rest fill from
'recent'. A pinned key is never lent to another project.
manual  exactly the slots you name. Unnamed keys stay dark.
mirror  the old behaviour - all six keys show the one winning state.
slots: six entries in AG00..AG05 order, each a directory or "", e.g.
Slots are STICKY: once a project holds a key it keeps it for as long as
it is live, however the activity order moves around it. Only a key that
has been vacated is refilled - so a glance always means the same thing.
`freemicro status` prints the current assignment.
